Setting alwaysApply: false in SKILL.md disables automatic skill injection into agent systems. For progressive-discovery, MCP server instructions alone (in instructions.py and progressive-discovery.md prompt) are sufficient without skill injection—useful pattern when skill behavior is stateless.

Rule blocking state during sessions originates from DB (already synced), not from source files. This prevents wasted debugging time trying to fix rule behavior by modifying source code when the actual blocking state is database-driven.

anthropic-agent-sdk-python SubprocessCLITransport._build_command: allowed_tools=[] and disallowed_tools=[] parameters don't work (truthiness check treats [] as falsy). Root cause: defaults use field(default_factory=list) instead of None, preventing is not None guards. Workaround: use None instead of []. Filed upstream as anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python#634

ClaudeAgentOptions instantiations require tools=[] parameter to pass validation and prevent 'allowed_tools without tools' errors. Apply to all usages across codebase regardless of whether tools are actually used.
_score_tasks() and _resolve_ready_tasks() in task_readiness.py are shared helpers used by both suggest_next_task() and suggest_next_tasks(); refactoring validated by 32 regression tests
tasks/_affected_files.py uses post-hoc file annotation via update_observed_files() (git diff-tree from commits) rather than pre-computation—design choice for parallel dispatch file tracking

suggest_next_tasks() in task_readiness.py performs greedy batch selection with file-conflict detection to prevent concurrent tasks from modifying overlapping files—this is a core constraint for parallel dispatch
SDK v0.1.20 `rate_limit_event` issue: upstream anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python#634 filed. User prefers call-site filtering over SDK monkey-patching to avoid SDK internal coupling.

User/validator explicitly rejected monkey-patch workaround in src/gobby/llm/sdk_compat.py. Preferred approach: fix at call sites in claude_streaming.py and chat_session.py rather than patching SDK internals. Current monkey-patch is temporary; plan to migrate to call-site fixes.

Upstream issue anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python#634 filed for rate_limit_event parsing error in SDK v0.1.20. SDK's parse_message() raises MessageParseError on unknown message types, killing async generator.

Parallel dispatch architecture (#9576): suggest_next_tasks (plural) in task_readiness.py uses greedy selection with file-conflict detection via TaskAffectedFileManager. Scoring extracted into _score_tasks and _resolve_ready_tasks helpers. dispatch_batch in spawn_agent/_factory.py uses asyncio.gather for concurrent spawning. update_observed_files in tasks/_affected_files.py annotates tasks with actually-changed files from task.commits (list of SHA strings on Task model). Orchestrator YAML uses suggest_next_tasks + dispatch_batch for capacity-aware parallel dispatch (max_concurrent - in_progress count). Tasks with no file annotations are treated as non-conflicting (optimistic).

dispatch_batch tests require explicit agent="default" parameter - tests defaulted to agent="developer" which failed _load_agent_body validation. When modifying dispatch_batch test setup, ensure agent parameter is explicitly set to match what _load_agent_body expects.

MagicMock objects fail isinstance() type checks in tests. Encountered in update_observed_files test where MagicMock used for ctx.task_manager failed isinstance(ctx.task_manager, LocalTaskManager) check. When testing code with isinstance() validation, configure mock with spec= parameter, use MagicMock(spec=TargetClass), or provide real object instance instead.
Skills with alwaysApply: true and injectionFormat: content are automatically injected as additional context via SessionStart hook, independent of database installation or other injection pathways.

progressive-discovery skill (src/gobby/install/shared/skills/progressive-discovery/SKILL.md) is injected twice: (1) via SessionStart hook as additional context because alwaysApply: true + injectionFormat: content, and (2) via MCP Server Instructions (proxy includes overlapping <tool_discovery>, <skills>, <common_mistakes>, <rules> sections). Both teach the same progressive disclosure pattern, creating redundant token consumption. Template and DB versions are identical.

MCP tool discovery in Gobby uses progressive disclosure: list_mcp_servers → list_tools → get_tool_schema → call_tool. Three progressive disclosure rules enforce this chain: require-servers-listed, require-server-listed-for-schema, require-schema-before-call (commit a3899b51).

Skill template frontmatter fields: name, description, category (e.g., 'core'), alwaysApply (boolean), injectionFormat, audience, format_overrides.autonomous. DB-installed skill records (e.g., skl-f0317007) preserve metadata structure exactly matching template source.

Glob search fails to locate skill templates in src/gobby/install/shared/skills/; use grep search instead. Skill template files are SKILL.md within skill-specific subdirectories (e.g., src/gobby/install/shared/skills/progressive-discovery/SKILL.md).

Default agent automatically injects skills into session context at initialization (no manual get_skill() calls needed). Injected skills appear in SessionStart message metadata with 'Injected' flag. SessionStart shows: 60 gobby-tagged rules, 20 variables, 16 skills for default agent.

Intermittent tmux title flakiness confirmed in CLI tooling but untracked; insufficient repro steps to file formal bug; escalate as task only if pattern emerges with specific trigger, timing correlation, and CLI version context
Rule/authorization system has no update or patch method—existing rules cannot be modified in place. To change rule constraints, must delete the existing rule and recreate it with new parameters.

synthesize_title_from_prompt() runs as background task and fails silently on LLM timeout with no retry/fallback, causing intermittent blank tmux titles. Failure only visible in logs as WARNING events (no user-visible error). Root cause identified via log analysis showing timeout failures.

Adding a new rule to Gobby requires both implementation AND registration in the whitelist-building mechanism (in rule registry or session initialization modules). Runtime addition to `_active_rule_names` works for current session but won't persist across session restarts.

`_active_rule_names` is a whitelist populated at session initialization that gates which rules are active. A new rule implementation will not be applied unless registered in this whitelist. If a rule exists in code but doesn't activate, check the whitelist mechanism in rule registry or session initialization modules.

agents/SKILL.md is an MCP API reference (covers spawn_agent, kill_agent, messaging APIs) and does NOT cover agent YAML file authoring — if agent YAML authoring documentation is needed, it requires a new skill or separate documentation rather than enhancement of agents/SKILL.md

Progressive discovery capability moved from default agent instructions (default.yaml) to skill-template-only. Skill file: src/gobby/install/shared/skills/progressive-discovery/SKILL.md with alwaysApply: false already set. Teams requiring progressive discovery must load the skill explicitly rather than relying on default agent behavior.

Memory review enforcement gap: System has tracking rules (track-pending-memory-review, clear-memory-review-on-create, suggest-memory-after-close) that manage state and nudge users, but lacks a STOP event rule to block workflow progression when review is pending. Implementing enforcement requires a new STOP rule distinct from tracking infrastructure.

Claude Agent SDK: use tools=[] (not allowed_tools=[]) to disable all built-in tools. The SDK's subprocess_cli.py uses `if self._options.tools is not None` (correct is-not-None check) for tools, but `if self._options.allowed_tools` (falsy truthiness check) for allowed_tools. Empty list [] is falsy so allowed_tools=[] silently does nothing. Filed upstream: anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python#634.

_active_rule_names is a session-start whitelist computed by resolve_rules_for_agent() in src/gobby/workflows/selectors.py. It's built from the agent definition's workflows.rules + rule_selectors, stored in session_variables, and used by _filter_by_active_rules() in the rule engine. Rules added after session start are NOT automatically picked up — the whitelist is frozen for the session's lifetime. To add a rule mid-session, manually append to _active_rule_names in session_variables.

Rules must be tagged with 'gobby' to be included by the default agent. The default agent definition (src/gobby/install/shared/agents/default.yaml) uses rule_selectors with include: ['tag:gobby']. Rules without this tag won't appear in _active_rule_names and won't be evaluated. The selector logic is in src/gobby/workflows/selectors.py _match_rule(), which reads from rule.tags (the row-level DB column), NOT from definition_json.tags.
Bug in create_rule API (task #9657, open): tags in definition_json not propagated to row-level tags column, breaking rule_selectors tag matching.

Rules require tag:gobby in definition to be included by default agent's rule_selectors. Rules created mid-session not picked up until restart because _active_rule_names functions as session-start whitelist.

In summarizer.py, _generate_summary_sdk() fails when tools parameter passed as list instead of empty list (SDK attempts tool invocation). Fix: set tools=[], simplify prompt instructions, set max_turns=1. Apply to both call sites per task #9652.

Title synthesis timeouts are caused by SDK v0.1.20 MessageParseError on rate_limit_event (and other unknown message types); fixed in PR #9652 via monkey-patch in src/gobby/llm/sdk_compat.py that returns None instead of raising exceptions

Title synthesis fails silently on timeout: synthesize_title_from_prompt runs asynchronously (background=True) with default 30s timeout. When LLM call exceeds timeout, no exception is raised, no fallback is generated, and the session displays without a title (missing from tmux display). To debug missing titles, search logs for timeout warnings in synthesize_title_from_prompt at the affected session's timestamp.

When agent doesn't 'recover' from Gobby's push block, hook dispatcher triggers `[tool-failure-recovery]` rule via `~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py --cli=claude --type=stop`. Push blocks are intended safety behavior (not tool failures), so no recovery action needed.
Gobby worker safety rule blocks agent-initiated git pushes (intentional behavior, not error). Merge agent handles pushing instead. User must manually execute `git push` to push code.

DB rule configuration drift occurs when manual DB modifications change enabled state without updating agent_scope filters; symptoms: rule applies to broader set of agents than intended; check both enabled AND agent_scope match template during debugging

agent_scope field on DB rules acts as access control: if missing, rule applies to ALL agents (including default agent); if specified (e.g., agent_scope: [developer, qa-reviewer]), rule only affects those agent types — critical for intended scope

Bandit B311 flags `random.uniform()` used for backoff jitter in `src/gobby/llm/claude.py` as a security issue. Suppress with `# nosec B311` inline comment — it's a false positive since it's not used for cryptographic purposes.

Existing workflow records may contain corrupted `workflow_type: 'workflow'` values due to the silent validation reset behavior in `update_workflow`. Requires database audit and migration script to fix affected records.

The `update_workflow` tool silently resets invalid `workflow_type` values to 'workflow' instead of raising validation errors. Valid workflow types are: rule, pipeline, agent. This silent failure masked data inconsistency when validation rules were updated.

Bandit B311 (cryptographically weak randomness) flags random.uniform() even when used for non-cryptographic backoff jitter timing. This is a false positive in this codebase's pre-commit hooks. Suppress with inline comment '# nosec: B311' on the jitter calculation line. Location: backoff/retry logic in src/gobby/llm/ area.

`_get_claimed_task_info()` (line 960 of `_session.py`) lacks DB fallback — relies entirely on ephemeral session variables. When variables drift (due to silent merge failures), it returns `None` even if task actually exists in DB. Proposed fix: query `tasks` table for `assignee == session_id AND status == 'in_progress'` when session variables empty.

Compact handoff truthiness filter at line 459 of `_session.py`: `task_handoff = {k: parent_vars[k] for k in _TASK_CLAIM_KEYS if parent_vars.get(k)}` — empty parent variables propagate forward instead of being reconciled, breaking the handoff chain and perpetuating stale state.

Silent merge failures in `claim_task()` (line 613) and `create_task()` (line 170) of `_session.py`: `add_claimed_task()` calls wrapped in try/except blocks logged only at debug level. Failures are swallowed silently, causing session variables to drift out of sync with DB state without visible errors.

Bandit B311 (random module usage) triggers false positive when used for backoff jitter in retry logic. Requires explicit suppression via `# nosec B311` comment or bandit config. Located in `src/gobby/llm/claude.py` around retry/backoff logic.
create_rule API doesn't populate row-level tags column. See task #9657
update_workflow API silently resets workflow_type instead of preserving it across updates. See task #9679
track-task-release function unconditionally clears task_claimed field, losing claim tracking. See task #9680

Workaround for MCP proxy string serialization bug: Use plain text in tool descriptions—remove newlines, replace em dashes/unicode escapes with plain ASCII equivalents (e.g., hyphens). Conditional formatting triggers string-wrapping behavior in proxy argument marshaling.

MCP proxy serialization bug: Tool descriptions with special characters (\n newlines, \u2014 unicode escapes, em dashes) cause the entire `arguments` object to serialize as a JSON string instead of remaining a dict, triggering Pydantic validation error with `input_type=str` instead of expected dict type in `call_tool`

The `_get_claimed_task_info` function lacks database fallback, causing claimed tasks to become invisible when in-memory state variables drift from actual database state. Add DB query fallback for resilience against state divergence.

The `track-task-release` rule unconditionally clears `task_claimed` on task close, creating a redundant cleanup mechanism—`close_task` function already handles multi-task cleanup correctly. Rule should be removed entirely to prevent task claimed state loss.

State drift pattern: claimed_tasks dict empty while task_claimed=true indicates either create_task not called with claim=True or add_claimed_task merge failed silently. Suggests DB fallback query needed in _get_claimed_task_info: query tasks where assignee=session_id AND status=in_progress.

_lifecycle.py:258 close_task() correctly implements multi-task claiming: uses remove_claimed_task() which only sets task_claimed=false when claimed_tasks dict becomes empty. This is the intended design but gets overwritten by track-task-release rule.

track-task-release rule unconditionally sets task_claimed=false after every close_task operation, overwriting correct code-based state management that preserves task_claimed=true for multi-task claiming scenarios. Rule should be removed or refactored—code in _lifecycle.py:258 already handles this correctly via remove_claimed_task().

Gate configuration uses two-layer system: YAML templates (require-error-triage.yaml, require-task-close.yaml) with default `enabled: false` are deployed to database, but runtime gate behavior depends on database records which can override template state. Template `enabled: false` doesn't disable a gate if DB record has `enabled: true`. Check both template files and DB when debugging gate issues.
Rule sync architecture: remove_claimed_task() function is the sole code-based authority for excluding claimed tasks from sync behavior. No alternative removal mechanisms exist.
Workflow type mapping: legacy types 'step' and 'workflow' both map to 'pipeline' value. Implementation uses VALID_WORKFLOW_TYPES set across _definitions.py, sync.py, and workflow_definitions.py

VALID_WORKFLOW_TYPES = {rule, variable, agent, pipeline}. Legacy types 'step' and 'workflow' map to 'pipeline' for backwards compat. This mapping exists in 3 locations: (1) _definitions.py update_workflow_definition, (2) sync.py sync_bundled_workflows, (3) storage/workflow_definitions.py import_from_yaml. The create() method in storage does NOT validate workflow_type — it accepts anything, which is fine for direct DB operations. The ToolProxyErrorCode enum uses INVALID_ARGUMENTS (not VALIDATION_ERROR) for argument errors.

The `update_workflow` tool previously silently reset invalid `workflow_type` values to 'workflow' instead of raising validation errors. Valid workflow types are now: rule, variable, pipeline, agent. The silent reset behavior was fixed; legacy types 'step' and 'workflow' now map to 'pipeline'.

Workflow configuration sync process drops `agent_scope` field when serializing to `definition_json` (affects both template and installed source rows). Root cause: sync serialization logic doesn't preserve the field. Impact: missing `agent_scope` causes overly restrictive default permissions, blocking all user pushes. Example: template YAML defined `agent_scope: [developer, qa-reviewer]` but didn't persist to database.

Gobby role-based push authorization: only the 'merge agent' is authorized to execute git push operations. Other agents (including the merge resolution agent) attempting to push will be blocked with an authorization error. User must either execute push manually or request merge agent delegation.
Both gobby-workflows and gobby-pipelines expose overlapping CRUD operations for pipeline definitions, creating a potential inconsistency between the two servers that may need architectural review.

Gobby-workflows and gobby-pipelines are separated by concern: gobby-workflows owns workflow definitions + rules state management (19 tools: CRUD, session vars, rules, cache), while gobby-pipelines owns execution layer with approval gates and runtime operations (10 tools: run_pipeline, approve_pipeline, reject_pipeline, get_pipeline_status). This split clarifies why both servers exist.
gobby-rules does not exist as a separate server—rule handling (list, get, toggle, create, delete rules) is integrated into gobby-workflows server.

gobby-workflows and gobby-pipelines have functional split: gobby-workflows owns workflow/pipeline definitions, rules engine, and session state management; gobby-pipelines owns runtime execution (run_pipeline, approve_pipeline, reject_pipeline) and approval gates. Both have overlapping CRUD capabilities suggesting potential consolidation.

API key resolution for hub providers must be wired in BOTH src/gobby/skills/registries.py AND src/gobby/skills/runner.py - providers will not function if wired in only one location. This is a gotcha when adding new hub providers.

Hub provider pattern: HubProvider abstract base in src/gobby/skills/hubs/, registered via factories in registries.py and runner.py, coordinated through HubManager. search_all() returns tuple[results, errors] not just results. Errors surfaced at MCP/HTTP/CLI/UI layers via hub_errors field in responses.

gobby-rules does not exist as a separate service; rules functionality is consolidated within gobby-workflows. The actual backend topology is two services: gobby-pipelines (pipeline execution) and gobby-workflows (workflow orchestration, rule evaluation, and pipeline coordination).

search_all() function suppresses errors from all hub providers (CLI not installed, missing API keys, nonexistent repos, unreachable APIs), returning false-positive {"success": true, "count": 0} instead of surfacing underlying failures
The `save_expansion_spec` tool is located on the `gobby-tasks` MCP server, not `gobby-worker`. This must be called via the gobby-tasks interface to work correctly.

The `[require-task-close]` rule in the stop hook (invoked via `hook_dispatcher.py --type=stop`) incorrectly checks tasks globally rather than scoped to the current session. This causes it to block session exit with error 'Rule enforced by Gobby: [require-task-close] Tasks still in_progress: (unknown)' when OTHER sessions have in_progress tasks. Framework-level defect tracked in bug #9726.

Pattern: Task management hooks in multi-session environments must explicitly filter by session_id to avoid cross-session interference. The require-task-close enforcement applies globally to all in_progress tasks unless scoped, potentially blocking shutdown when unrelated sessions have pending tasks.

Bug #9726: hook_dispatcher.py's require-task-close rule enforcement is not session-aware—it aggregates in_progress tasks across session boundaries instead of filtering by session_id. The rule checks task closure globally rather than scoping to the current session, causing stop hook blocking on tasks from different sessions. File: hook_dispatcher.py.

Each agent session has a unique session ID (format: alphanumeric hash, e.g., 427ce548..., f296f433...). Stop hook validation should filter tasks by session ID ownership rather than treating all project in_progress tasks as blocking constraints.

Stop hook `require-task-close` validation rule blocks on ALL in_progress tasks globally without session-awareness, causing false positives when parallel sessions have open tasks. Bug filed in #9726. Fix requires filtering validation logic by session ID to only block on tasks claimed by the exiting session.

When multiple parallel agent sessions run simultaneously, unrelated in_progress tasks from other sessions can block the current session's task closure via require-task-close rule. Check task session context (commit refs) to distinguish external blocks.

Stop hook rule `require-task-close` lacks session-aware filtering - checks all in_progress tasks globally instead of scoping to current agent session. Causes false task closure blocks in multi-session scenarios (bug #9726).

Stop hook mechanism bug (task #9726): incorrectly blocks when encountering in_progress tasks assigned to different agent sessions. The hook should only validate tasks belonging to the current session. This causes gate blockage when other sessions have in_progress work, leading to auto-resolution after max retry attempts.

Gobby stop hook (`require-task-close`) can get stuck in a loop when it detects in_progress tasks belonging to OTHER sessions that the current agent cannot close. The hook checks all in_progress tasks globally, not just the current session's tasks. Filed as bug #9726.
Workaround for stop hook [require-task-close] block: the stop gate will auto-open after max retry attempts, eventually allowing shutdown to proceed despite the blocking rule.

Stop hook [require-task-close] rule in hook_dispatcher.py doesn't isolate tasks by session—it can block shutdown by reporting in_progress tasks from OTHER sessions, even when current session's tasks are all closed. Located in stop hook mechanism.

The stop hook has a logic bug: it queries all in_progress tasks in the project rather than filtering by current session, causing false-positive blocks when parallel agent sessions have active work. When debugging task status change blocks, check if the blocking in_progress tasks belong to a different session before assuming the current session's work is incomplete.
Stop hook implements exponential backoff retry with up to 3 attempts for `[require-task-close]` validation errors - transient failures may resolve automatically without manual intervention.

Stop hook `[require-task-close]` error may be triggered by in_progress tasks from concurrent agent sessions - task detection lacks session filtering. When encountering this error, check if reported task IDs (e.g., #9722, #9716) belong to the current agent session before investigating.

hook_dispatcher.py --cli=claude --type=stop command has [require-task-close] rule that incorrectly flags in_progress tasks from other sessions as belonging to the current session; hook auto-retries automatically; issue resolves when current session's own tasks are properly closed

When stop hook blocks session exit due to in_progress tasks, query the task system to verify session ownership of reported tasks (e.g., task #9722 belonged to session f296f433..., not current session). Cross-session tasks don't block the current session's exit - the gate issue is a state filtering bug, not a true blocker.

The `require-task-close` gate enforcement has a bug where it incorrectly flags in_progress tasks from external sessions as blocking the current session's graceful shutdown. The stop hook retries 3 times (0/3 → 1/3 → 2/3) with automatic state re-evaluation, so closing the current session's own tasks typically satisfies the gate on later retry attempts despite initial false positives from stale cross-session task visibility.

Pre-existing lint errors in test files: B017 (bare Exception) at test_skills_coverage.py:933,941 tracked in task #9724; I001 (import sort) at test_skills_routes.py:3 tracked in task #9725. Not blocking for development — separate from current implementation work.

GitHub Collection SKILL.md discovery logic: probes first directory for SKILL.md, then recurses one level deep for skills/<category>/<skill>/SKILL.md pattern. Implemented in search_all() for GitHub hub type.

SkillHub provider was removed and replaced with SkillsMP provider targeting REST API at skillsmp.com/api/v1. Default hub list is now: anthropic-skills, claude-plugins, clawdhub, skillsmp. HubConfig.type Literal changed from 'skillhub' to 'skillsmp'.
search_all() API changed to return (results, errors) tuple instead of silently swallowing per-hub exceptions. Callers must now handle both results and errors separately.
Gobby background processing/expansion operations typically take 60-90 seconds to complete. Relevant when initiating async work or waiting for state changes.

Gobby's `require-task-close` pre-flight hook validation rule blocks hook operations (e.g., `stop` type) if any tasks remain in `in_progress` state. This enforces task lifecycle discipline and prevents uncontrolled exits with dangling work items. Located in Gobby's hook validation logic.
When Gobby agent stop command fails with task blocking error, resolution is: complete task work, commit changes, then execute close_task() on all in_progress tasks before retry

Gobby's stop hook (hook_dispatcher.py --cli=claude --type=stop) enforces [require-task-close] validation rule: prevents agent shutdown until all in_progress tasks are closed via close_task() call and changes committed

Task #9716 (test battery) remains in_progress due to external pipeline dependency pe-18a2d880. The require-task-close governance rule in hook_dispatcher.py was intentionally overridden—long-running test tasks with external blockers are valid exceptions to task closure enforcement.
SkillsMP is REST API provider at skillsmp.com/api/v1 with 350K+ skills catalog; requires api_keys parameter wired from environment through registries.py and runner.py
search_all() in registries.py now returns (results, errors) tuple instead of silently swallowing per-hub exceptions - breaking change for callers
Researcher agents spawned as background workers legitimately block on wait steps during normal operation—this is expected behavior and does not indicate an error or stuck process.

The `require-task-close` rule in Gobby enforces that tasks in `in_progress` state must be committed and closed before the stop hook can execute. Attempting to stop the process while a task is uncommitted will be blocked by this rule enforcement.

Implement MCP tools `wait_for_pipeline(execution_id)` and `wait_for_agent(run_id)` that call `completion_registry.wait()` internally. These block until completion event fires and return result, keeping session actively occupied to prevent stop hook triggers during orchestrated pipeline waits.

Stop hook repeatedly fires when agent is idle waiting for long-running pipeline with open task (e.g., task #9716 waiting for pipeline pe-af897b40). Root cause: stop hook enforces [require-task-close] rule during idle gaps. Solution: implement blocking wait tools to eliminate idle gaps and keep session actively occupied.

The session gate pending_memory_review can be cleared via set_variable() by passing pending_memory_review=False after memory review is completed. This prevents the stop hook from re-enforcing the [require-memory-review] rule on the next stop attempt.

For long-running async pipelines like expand-task with researcher agents, configure async event-based completion notification instead of polling. Pass async event config when spawning the pipeline and wait for the completion event (e.g., pe-af897b40) rather than polling status. This is more resource-efficient and responsive than polling loops.

For async expansion pipelines in Gobby, keep the orchestrating task in `in_progress` state as a checkpoint rather than closing it. Use event-driven notification to await pipeline completion instead of polling. Override `[require-task-close]` stop hooks when the workflow requires the task to remain open during async execution (e.g., task #9716 was kept open pending pipeline pe-af897b40 completion).

When using expansion-task pipelines (e.g., `pe-af897b40`), keep the initiating task (e.g., #9716) in `in_progress` state during async execution. Gobby's `require-task-close` stop hook fires preemptively but doesn't understand pending async dependencies. Decline the gate and explain the task lifecycle to maintain context until pipeline completion.
Researcher agent for expansion work is spawned as separate process to parallelize with main agent workflow - pattern for delegating expansion task generation/requirement breakdown
Expansion pipelines use event-based completion notification instead of polling - completion signal sent asynchronously rather than client polling for status

StepRenderer component fails to evaluate template expressions in wait step type. When using template syntax (e.g., ${variable}) in wait steps, expressions are not processed. Task #9720 tracks this issue.

Pipeline completion uses push-based event notification via `completion_registry.wait()` blocking, not polling. The executor blocks on this call and automatically notifies the session when dependent tasks complete. Do not implement periodic status polling.

Pipeline wait steps use the CompletionEventRegistry for push-based notification — do NOT poll with sleep loops. The pipeline executor blocks on completion_registry.wait() and the session gets woken when the agent finishes. StepRenderer must render ALL step type fields (exec, prompt, mcp, invoke_pipeline, wait) — the wait field was missing rendering until commit 96373102.

The 'stop' hook is configured in critical_hooks list in hook_dispatcher.py (src/gobby/install/shared/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py and ~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py) and receives special treatment in the hook system.
STOP gate diagnostic logging in src/gobby/workflows/rule_engine.py should include pre_existing_errors_triaged and error_triage_blocks fields for complete visibility into rule enforcement decisions.

The STOP gate diagnostic logging in `src/gobby/workflows/rule_engine.py` includes `pre_existing_errors_triaged` and `error_triage_blocks` fields that provide improved visibility into error tracking and triage status during rule evaluation.

In `src/gobby/install/shared/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py`, the string `"stop"` must be included in Claude's `critical_hooks` list to ensure stop-gate errors are properly handled and prioritized by the hook dispatcher.

For Gobby graph-RAG: reject Neo4j and Qdrant (external server processes with health checks, coordination, config overhead inappropriate for laptop-local daemon). Use SQLite-based single-process architecture instead.
Gobby's `require-error-triage` stop-hook validates changes by type and exempts documentation-only modifications from lint/test requirements—gates only enforce validation for executable code changes.
MCP implementation plan for gobby codebase documented in docs/plans/gobby-codebase-mcp.md - comprehensive planning complete and ready to guide implementation work

Graph-RAG codebase indexing plan is at docs/plans/gobby-codebase-mcp.md. Key architecture decisions: reuse existing VectorStore with collection_name='code_chunks', reuse existing Neo4jClient with Code-prefixed labels (CodeFile, CodeClass, CodeFunction, _CodeSymbol) to separate from memory graph, tree-sitter for parsing (required dep, not optional), new module at src/gobby/codebase/, MCP server gobby-codebase with tools: index_project, search_code, get_symbol, get_callers, get_callees, get_file_symbols, get_dependencies, index_status. UI lives under Projects > Code tab (enhanced with Browse/Search/Graph modes), not a separate sidebar tab. Tasks and Sessions tabs removed from project detail view (redundant with sidebar).
Claude Code CLI MCP connector configuration is located in ~/.claude/settings.json; authentication can also be managed via Claude Code UI

'2 claude.ai connectors need auth' is a Claude Code CLI notification (not Gobby-related) indicating MCP servers configured through Anthropic's connector infrastructure require OAuth token refresh or initial authentication setup

`gobby restart` kills daemon and starts new `HookManager` instance with empty cache. Sessions persist in DB; no state cleanup required. Subsequent events trigger `session_lookup.resolve()` to query session UUID from database. Design correctly handles variable lookup across restarts.

Race condition pattern: After daemon restart via `gobby restart`, STOP events can fire via `WorkflowHookHandler` before `BEFORE_AGENT` event runs `session_lookup()` to populate `HookManager` cache. Gate conditions then can't access session variables from DB. Issue: cache not populated, not DB persistence failure.

Session variables stored as integer 1 (not boolean true). Gate conditions using `not variables.get('pre_existing_errors_triaged')` evaluate correctly because Python: `not 1 == False`. Affects any boolean flags stored in session variables.

Tree-sitter is a required (not optional) dependency for codebase indexing. Default grammars included: Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Go, Rust. Optional additional grammars: Ruby, Java, C/C++, C# installable via pip for specialized users.

Project detail view consolidated all code browsing capabilities (Browse/Search/Graph modes) into a single unified 'Code' tab. Tasks and Sessions tabs removed. No App.tsx or sidebar routing changes required.
Planning workflow entry point: gobby-planning skill/pipeline
Planning agent framework output directory: .gobby/planning/

Gobby planning agent framework: 6 agents (gobby-discovery, gobby-product, gobby-ux, gobby-architect, gobby-test, gobby-docs) with 9 skills (gobby-brainstorm, gobby-research, gobby-brief, gobby-prd, gobby-prd-review, gobby-ux-design, gobby-architecture, gobby-test-strategy, gobby-documentation). Routing model: agent name = skill tag. Each agent selects skills tagged with matching domain.
Setup wizard logic is delegated to a separate Node app rather than being part of core initialization
Gobby's init flow already validates for tmux presence with appropriate warnings

In naming convention updates, prose documentation references to 'planning' (in text/explanations) were intentionally left unchanged as semantically appropriate. Only configuration names, pipeline names, and skill identifiers were migrated to gobby-* namespace. Don't over-update prose text.

Agent-to-skill routing pattern: agent name = skill tag prefix. Example: gobby-discovery agent routes to skills tagged [gobby-discovery]. Use this for adding new agents/skills to maintain consistent navigation through the planning system.

Gobby naming convention final state: Agents: gobby-discovery, gobby-product, gobby-ux, gobby-architect, gobby-test, gobby-docs. Skills: gobby-brainstorm, gobby-research, gobby-brief, gobby-prd, gobby-prd-review, gobby-ux-design, gobby-architecture, gobby-test-strategy, gobby-documentation. Skill tags: [gobby-*] matching agent names. Pipeline and entry point: both named `gobby-planning`. Output directory: `.gobby/planning`.
Hub evaluation pattern: filtering for 'no-auth hubs' — publicly accessible APIs without authentication requirements as primary selection criterion
`anthropics/skills` hub exists and is a viable no-auth hub candidate (discovered during recent investigation)
SkillPort hub is located at repository `gotalab/skillport`

Sidebar navigation already has 14+ tabs; codebase context features are project-scoped and should not be added as top-level sidebar items. All codebase features belong in ProjectDetailView to avoid navigation bloat.

ProjectDetailView.tsx contains an existing 'Code' tab that uses FilesPage component for file browsing. Codebase features (search, graph visualization, index status indicator) should be integrated within this existing Code tab as multiple view modes (Browse/Search/Graph), not as separate sidebar tabs.

After daemon restart, hook dispatcher must re-resolve CLI external session IDs to platform session IDs via SESSION_START handler in `session_lookup.py` before other events fire. Events firing before this re-mapping is established operate on unresolved session context.

Session variable persistence works correctly across daemon restarts via `hooks.py:117` (load) and `hooks.py:139` (persist) using SQLite `session_variables` table. If variables appear lost after restart, the issue is session ID resolution timing in `session_lookup.py`, not data loss.
The `pre_existing_errors_triaged` variable is cleared on daemon restart as expected behavior, resetting error-triage tracking state with each restart. This is normal session state reset, not a bug.

The `[require-error-triage]` Gobby rule blocks daemon stop operations via `hook_dispatcher.py --cli=claude --type=stop`, requiring lint and tests to pass before shutdown. This gate ensures uncommitted errors are properly triaged before daemon stops.

To clear `require-error-triage` gate: call `set_variable(name="pre_existing_errors_triaged", value=true)` after verifying test suite passes and linting is clean. This satisfies the pre-stop triage requirement and allows daemon shutdown.

Gobby's `hook_dispatcher.py` enforces `require-error-triage` gate on stop operations: daemon shutdown is blocked until pre-existing errors are triaged (either fixed or explicitly verified). Gate must be cleared before `--type=stop` command succeeds.
Neo4jClient is reusable across different entity labels without modification - code entity separation can leverage Neo4j label abstraction as viable path without Neo4jClient refactoring
VectorStore is parameterized with `collection_name` and already supports multi-collection instantiation - code collection can be added as separate instance without architectural refactoring

In `rule_engine.py:121`, a hard-reset `variables["pre_existing_errors_triaged"] = False` on every BEFORE_AGENT event prevented gates from maintaining state between events, making the require-error-triage gate unsatisfiable. Removing this reset line was the critical fix in task #9713.

require-error-triage.yaml implements 3-strike circuit breaker via compound effects: set_variable action (incrementing error_triage_blocks) listed before block action; gate opens when condition error_triage_blocks < 3 evaluates true after 3 consecutive blocks

rule_engine.py line 121 contained erroneous variable reset: variables["pre_existing_errors_triaged"] = False on BEFORE_AGENT events, hard-resetting the triage flag on every event and making the gating condition unsatisfiable. Fix: remove this line.

BEFORE_AGENT event handlers: unconditional flag resets that execute before gate evaluation can silently break gating logic. Resets must either be conditional or deferred until after gate conditions are checked.

In require-error-triage.yaml circuit breaker config: compound effects ordering is critical - `set_variable` (incrementing error_triage_blocks) must execute before `block` action in the effects chain to properly track state. Gate opens after 3 consecutive blocks (condition: error_triage_blocks < 3).

rule_engine.py line 121 had a critical bug: `variables["pre_existing_errors_triaged"] = False` was executing unconditionally on every BEFORE_AGENT event before gate evaluation, blocking the error triage gate from ever being satisfied. Reset must occur after gate state is evaluated.
Use py-tree-sitter for language-aware symbol extraction in graph-RAG (extracts functions, classes, methods, modules with spans and signatures to populate code_nodes table).
Use sqlite-vec (SQLite vector extension) for graph-RAG vector storage, not Qdrant. Leverages existing memory/embeddings.py infrastructure and enables single-process design.

Pattern for gated attempt-limited blocking: pair 'when' condition checking variable count (error_triage_blocks < 3) with set_variable effect incrementing via {{ variables.get('error_triage_blocks', 0) + 1 }} before block effect in same rule

Rule system effect ordering: non-block effects (set_variable, etc.) execute BEFORE block effects, enabling deferred block pattern (rule code lines 250-253)—allows single rule to increment counter via set_variable before block evaluation
Line 121 in the rule system contains a hard-reset that clears counter variables, preventing persistence of attempt counts—must be removed when implementing gated blocking features like error-triage

_tool_pool is a local variable at line 168 within _run_with_sdk() function, not module-level. This is a scope distinction in the Claude Agent SDK compatibility layer (src/gobby/llm/sdk_compat.py or related SDK modules).

pre_existing_errors_triaged variable state cycle: agent sets true → user message triggers BEFORE_AGENT → resets to False → stop hook checks and blocks again. stop_attempts configuration gates condition (< max threshold); after 8+ attempts, escalation gate opens.

Deprecated reset-stop-cycle-on-prompt.yaml implemented correct pattern: variable lifecycle managed by YAML rules, not Python code. Future fixes should restore rule-based reset behavior instead of Python event handler resets.

Line 121 in rule engine's BEFORE_AGENT event handler hard-resets pre_existing_errors_triaged = False on every user prompt, breaking variable persistence across prompt cycles. Variable should persist until explicitly reset by rules, not by event handlers.

Infinite loop mitigation: use `stop_attempts` counter as circuit breaker when fixing discovered bugs. Allow maximum 3 fix attempts before escalating to priority-1 task with context. Prevents agents from getting stuck on unfixable bugs.

Multi-agent error-handling convention: when discovering lint/test failures, check `git diff --name-only` to determine code origin. Uncommitted code (WIP from another agent) → create bug task and move on. Committed code → agent owns fix, must fix in place and commit. Set `pre_existing_errors_triaged=true` after verification passes.

Triage rule anti-pattern in Gobby workflow: 'Fix bugs or confirm none' creates path-of-least-resistance non-compliance not from agent laziness but from lack of verification feedback loop. Fix requires: (1) prescriptive steps instead of vague instructions (e.g., `ruff check`, `pytest` explicitly), (2) distinction between 'bugs introduced by my changes' (must fix) vs 'pre-existing issues' (create task), (3) remove easy escapes by requiring verification before confirmation. Consider MCP-based automated verification to make assertions checkable rather than self-reported.

Gobby's `require-error-triage` enforcement rule blocks session termination and all subsequent actions (commits, file modifications, task closures) until error triage is complete. Agent must run CI/test commands to identify pre-existing failures, then either fix failures in touched code or explicitly set `set_variable(name="pre_existing_errors_triaged", value=true)` to confirm no orthogonal fixable errors exist.

Variable propagation delay in hook_dispatcher.py: calling set_variable() may complete before the hook evaluator sees the updated state, causing rule re-evaluation to occur with stale variable values—may require retry or explicit wait

The `require-error-triage` rule in hook_dispatcher.py blocks session stops via stop hook until `set_variable(name="pre_existing_errors_triaged", value=true)` is called to mark error triage as complete

Planned: 3 YAML agent definition files (role, personality, instructions).

gobby-artifacts is dead — no implementation, classes, or MCP tool bindings exist. Artifacts storage is filesystem-based instead (configurable path, e.g., _bmad-output/). Do not spend time looking for gobby-artifacts code or integration points.

The reopen_task() function clears the `claimed_tasks` session variable when reopening a task and sets the session-task link action field to 'reopened'. This ensures claiming state consistency. Location in codebase not specified in current session record.

session_end.md template includes DB context frontmatter fields: claimed_tasks, session_memories, first_digest_turn, recent_digest_turns. These variables are rendered in the template body to provide database context for session-end summaries.

Gobby's summarization pipeline requires synchronized updates across three files: compact.md (concise prompt), session_end.md (session summary template), and summarize.py (code). Both compact and full summaries must have equal access to claimed_tasks, session_memories, and digest_turn data. In summarize.py, _generate_full_summary() must call _get_claimed_tasks(), _get_session_memories(), _extract_digest_turns() and pass results in context dict to match compact summary capabilities.

In claude_executor.py, _tool_pool (ThreadPoolExecutor) is a LOCAL variable created inside _run_with_sdk(), not a module-level attribute. It's created fresh per execution at line ~168. Fixed to use finally: _tool_pool.shutdown(wait=False) for proper cleanup.

sqlite3.Row objects do NOT support .get() method. Use `if "key" in row.keys()` pattern instead. Attempting row.get("key", default) raises AttributeError. This was discovered when trying to simplify pipeline_state.py from_row() methods — the change had to be reverted.

When stop hook enforces require-task-close, verify task status in database before committing/closing—may be bug #9711 false positive from stale claimed entries. Pattern occurs with completed non-code work (expansion, documentation) where zero code diffs exist.

Bug #9711: Stop hook require-task-close rule produces false positives from stale `claimed` database entries. Example: Task #9214 detected as claimed despite actual open status with no assignee and zero code diffs.

Session-task link state can diverge from actual task status—reopen_task operation clears task fields but doesn't update the corresponding session-task link record. Link persists stale action: 'claimed' while task becomes status: 'open' with assignee: null. Session stop hook checks link's action field instead of actual task status, causing incorrect behavior. Bug #9711 filed.

BMAD master pipeline: 4-phase structure: Phase 1 (Analysis, optional) → Phase 2 (Planning, PRD required) → Phase 3 (Solutioning, architecture+epics required) → Phase 4 (Implementation loop: create-story→validate→dev→code-review with loop-back on review failure).

BMAD pipeline architecture decision: Use `spawn_agent` MCP tool to invoke agents from pipelines rather than embedding workflow instructions in prompts—preserves full persona, memory, and autonomy per agent.

BMAD → Gobby integration: Create 11 agents with mappings: `bmad-analyst` (Mary), `bmad-pm` (John), `bmad-architect` (Winston), `bmad-ux-designer` (Sally), `bmad-dev` (Amelia), `bmad-sm` (Bob), `bmad-qa` (Quinn), `bmad-tech-writer` (Paige), `bmad-quick-flow` (Barry), `bmad-tea` (Murat), `bmad-master` (Murat). Seed memory via gobby-memory facts with `inject_context` rules.

AgentStepsEditor catch block correctly handles parse errors during real-time JSON editing (expected behavior when editing unterminated JSON). The actual bug requiring fixes was in tool_proxy, not AgentStepsEditor—prevents wasted debugging effort on the wrong component.

hook_dispatcher.py --type=stop enforce of require-task-close rule may have stale state information and incorrectly flag tasks that have already been transitioned (e.g., reopened from in_progress to open after planning phase). Check actual task status directly in task record before accepting validation error.

Gobby parent tasks should remain open and unclaimed during subtask execution, not closed. When require-task-close rule triggers on a parent task, unclaim it (don't close/commit) to maintain parent-child context while allowing subtasks to proceed independently.

System architecture constraint: Gobby stores system variables as flat SQLite dicts with no read-time computed properties. This architectural limitation is why task_claimed uses write-time materialization instead of being computed at read-time.

task_claimed is write-time computed in src/gobby/task_claim_state.py: add_claimed_task() returns {"task_claimed": True, ...} and remove_claimed_task() returns {"task_claimed": len(tasks) > 0, ...}. It's ergonomic sugar for YAML rule authors but redundant with claimed_tasks dict truthiness. Has ~30+ references across rules, agents, workflows, and Python code—this reference count blocks removal.
task_claimed boolean appears in ~30+ locations across YAML rules, workflows, and Python modules - high blast radius if attempting removal/refactoring
claimed_tasks dict is required for template rendering in task_claim_state.py line 13 (effect.reason template) - removing it breaks template output even if task_claimed boolean remains
task_claim_state.py: task_claimed (bool) and claimed_tasks (dict uuid→task_num) are always synchronized via add_claimed_task()/remove_claimed_task() methods - they form an inseparable state pair

In task_claim_state.py: `task_claimed` (boolean) and `claimed_tasks` (dict) are always synchronized by add_claimed_task()/remove_claimed_task(). Checking both in YAML `when` condition is redundant—if task_claimed is truthy, claimed_tasks is guaranteed non-empty. But claimed_tasks still required as template variable for effect.reason (renders task list).

SESSION_START 120s blocking wait is intentional design: parent session summary must be injected as context before LLM execution during session handoff. 120s is worst-case timeout; typical waits are short after BEFORE_AGENT triggers summary generation. Not a bug.

In SESSION_START handler, summary generation typically starts at BEFORE_AGENT hook, so actual blocking wait is usually much shorter than 120s worst-case timeout. Clarifies that the blocking time is not as problematic as the full timeout might suggest.

SESSION_START blocking (120s timeout) is intentional architectural design for session handoff. Parent's summary must be injected as context before LLM execution when session starts via `/clear` or `/compact` endpoints. Non-blocking approach would allow LLM to run before summary is available, defeating the handoff mechanism. CodeRabbit suggested making this non-blocking but was rejected.
llm/cost_table.py contains hard-coded invalid model keys flagged during codebase triage; pending validation and remediation

Stop hook rule `require-error-triage` enforced via `~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py`: before task completion, agent must triage any pre-existing errors and set variable `pre_existing_errors_triaged=true` to pass validation.
Josh always wants pushback when I disagree with something. Never just agree for the sake of agreement — voice concerns and suggest alternatives. This is a strong user preference.

During error triage, distinguish between pre-existing errors (orthogonal to task scope) and task-specific errors—only task-assigned errors require fixes; pre-existing issues can be confirmed as 'triaged' rather than fixed to satisfy the validation gate.

SESSION_START 120s blocking wait is intentional design, not a bug: parent session summary must be injected as context before LLM execution during session handoff. 120s is worst-case timeout; typical waits are short because BEFORE_AGENT hook triggers parent summary generation earlier. CodeRabbit suggested making this non-blocking but was rejected.
In _session.py line 978, use `return result or None` (not just `return result`) to maintain consistency with the empty list guard pattern at line 996.
Bundled variable count in _session.py is 18 (not 15). Test assertions in test_session_defaults.py require updating to expect 18 variables. This code/test mismatch was resolved in commit 679f5d63.

Validation hook `require-commit-before-close` blocks closure of tasks with no direct commits, including parent epics. Use `skip_validation` parameter with override justification to close parent epics.

Parent/rollup epics (#9578 Orchestrator v3, #9683 Bug fixes batch) have no direct commits by design—they only coordinate and merge child task branches. This is a legitimate structural pattern, not incomplete work.
After running `uv sync` for dependency updates, restart the daemon service to pick up changes.
`authlib` is a transitive dependency pulled in by `fastmcp` (not directly in `pyproject.toml`). Located in `uv.lock`. Upgrade via `uv lock --upgrade-package authlib`.

task_has_commits flag is set by detect_commit_link observer and required by 4 stop gates: require-error-triage, require-commit-before-close, block-skip-validation-with-commit, require-memory-review. If these gates fail unexpectedly, check detect_commit_link observer for commit detection issues (observer was completely missing in codebase before #9684).

Stop hook failed during hook_dispatcher.py execution (Epic #9577 session, Exchange 7). Tool failure recovery enforced per [tool-failure-recovery] rule—execution continued without stopping. Indicates potential fragility in hook_dispatcher.py stop hook handling.

Rule engine `task_has_commits` variable doesn't initialize until commit-tracking fires; remains null if no commits occur in session, causing dependent gate conditions (like `error-triage`) to evaluate false even when gate is installed/enabled

Known issue: task_has_commits variable fails to populate to true despite commits being made during session. This prevents require-error-triage from firing even when pre_existing_errors_triaged is true. Root cause is upstream in commit-tracking mechanism, not gate logic.
require-error-triage gate requires BOTH pre_existing_errors_triaged=true AND task_has_commits=true to fire. If either is null/false, the entire condition fails and the next-priority gate evaluates.
Rule engine gate priority system: lower priority number = higher priority = evaluates first. require-error-triage (priority 30) evaluates before require-task-close (priority 50).

ClawdHub CLI actual command flags differ from provider expectations: `--version` is actually `--cli-version` or `-V`; `search`, `install` commands don't support `--json` output; `info` command doesn't exist; only `explore` reliably supports JSON output.

ClawdHub CLI: `explore` command (with `--json` flag support) is for remote registry browsing/searching; `list` command is for local-only package listing and doesn't support `--json` or `--limit` flags. Provider was conflating these two endpoints.

Rule synchronization bug: the rule sync pipeline does not persist `agent_scope` from YAML template definitions into the `definition_json` column in the database. This causes installed rule copies to lose scope information, resulting in rules applying too broadly (e.g., `no-push-for-workers` was blocking all agents instead of only `["developer", "qa-reviewer"]`). Requires investigation in the rule sync pipeline.

After running `uv sync`, `uv.lock` regenerates and shows as modified in git status. This is normal uv package manager behavior during dependency resolution. Solution: commit the modified `uv.lock` file to achieve clean git state rather than attempting to prevent regeneration.

SkillsMP provider requires SKILLSMP_API_KEY environment variable. Missing key causes raw HTTP 401 errors. Solution: implement early auth guards in search() and list_skills() methods to provide user-friendly error message 'API key not configured' before API requests.

ClawHub npm package renamed from 'clawdhub' to 'clawhub' v0.7.0. CLI commands changed: version detection uses '--cli-version' (not '--version'), list skills uses 'explore --json' (not 'list'), get details uses 'inspect --json' (not 'info'), search doesn't support '--json' flag (parse text output instead).

ClawHub CLI: Package renamed from `clawdhub` to `clawhub` (npm). Current version: v0.7.0. Key CLI differences from what was originally coded:
- Version flag: `--cli-version` or `-V` (not `--version`)
- `search` has no `--json` output — text only, format: `slug  vX.Y.Z  summary`
- `explore --json` for browsing remote registry (not `list` which is local lockfile only)
- `inspect <slug> --json` for skill details (not `info` which doesn't exist)
- `install <slug>` has no `--json`, uses `--force` for non-interactive
- Registry: clawdhub.com / clawhub.ai
- Docs: https://github.com/openclaw/clawhub/blob/main/docs/cli.md

Gobby has a 'require-task-close' enforcement rule that can block task closure and prevent stop sequences (ctrl+o). This rule is enforced by the hook dispatcher at ~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py. When tasks remain in 'in_progress' status, this rule may prevent closing or exiting.

The `gobby-pipelines` module has been eliminated as of release 0.2.25. Agent definition CRUD operations were migrated to `gobby-workflows`. See commit 76beec97 for MCP proxy consolidation that enabled this cleanup.

Ruff's import sorting rule (`I` rule) rejects lazy imports in try-except blocks unless wrapped in parentheses. File runner.py contains examples. Solution: wrap multi-line imports in parentheses per Python standard, e.g., `from module import (Item1, Item2)` instead of split lines. Check both the obvious and secondary lazy import locations when fixing.

MCP proxy server consolidation (0.2.25): gobby-pipelines eliminated, agent definition CRUD moved from gobby-agents to gobby-workflows. gobby-workflows is now the umbrella server (37 tools) covering: workflow CRUD, rules, variables, pipeline CRUD+execution, agent definition CRUD. gobby-agents is runtime-only (spawn, lifecycle, messaging). Key files: workflows/__init__.py (registry factory accepts pipeline deps), workflows/_agents.py (agent def CRUD), workflows/_pipelines.py (pipeline tool registration), workflows/_pipeline_execution.py and _pipeline_discovery.py (moved from pipelines/). The old pipelines/ package and agent_definitions.py were deleted.
